references.notes: The phrase "أقسم ربها" (her lord swore) refers to an oath made by a powerful figure, implicitly invoking a sacred entity or principle, common in pre-Islamic Arabian society.
"وجدك" is an oath formula, swearing by one's ancestor or fortune, a common practice in pre-Islamic Arabia.
"الصدي" refers to the pre-Islamic belief of a bird-like entity emerging from the skull of the deceased, crying for drink.
"الدهر" (Time/Fate) is invoked as a cosmological force responsible for decay and destruction, a significant concept in pre-Islamic Arabian thought.
"لعمرك" (By your life) is a common pre-Islamic oath, invoking the sacredness of life.
"وجدك" is an oath formula, swearing by one's ancestor or fortune, a common practice in pre-Islamic Arabia.
The phrase "لو شاء ربي" (if my Lord willed) invokes the will of a divine entity, a common expression of divine agency in pre-Islamic poetry.
"آليت" (I swore/vowed) refers to the act of making an oath, a ritualistic practice in pre-Islamic society.
"فانعنيني" (lament me) refers to the practice of lamentation and mourning rites for the deceased, a key aspect of pre-Islamic mortuary customs.
"لعمرك" (By your life) is a common pre-Islamic oath, invoking the sacredness of life.
This verse refers to 'al-manāyā' (fates/deaths) as an active, personified force, which is a key cosmological concept in pre-Islamic Arabian belief regarding destiny and the inevitability of death.

This verse alludes to the destruction of ancient peoples like 'Ad and past generations, a common pre-Islamic motif reflecting the inexorable power of fate or time, aligning with cosmological concepts of destiny and the ephemeral nature of human existence.
The phrase 'his stars disappeared' refers to the pre-Islamic belief that stars were linked to an individual's fate or destiny, signifying the end of life or fortune.
The term 'nawādib' (lamenters) explicitly refers to women who performed ritual lamentation for the deceased, a significant pre-Islamic mortuary practice.
The verse contains an imprecation invoking 'Allah,' a common practice in pre-Islamic Arabian religion where the supreme deity was called upon in oaths and curses.
The term 'abqarī refers to something of extraordinary quality, often implying a supernatural origin or connection to the jinn, who were believed to inhabit the legendary land of 'Abqar.

Al-Muʿallā and al-Safīḥ are names of specific arrows used in the pre-Islamic game of maysir, a form of ritualized gambling and sortilege often associated with divination and the distribution of sacrificial meat.
The phrase 'والله' (by God) is an explicit oath formula, common in pre-Islamic Arabian speech and poetry, invoking a sacred entity.
This verse explicitly mentions 'al-Jinn' (the jinn), which were widely believed to be supernatural entities in pre-Islamic Arabian culture.
The phrase "حبل المنية" (rope of fate/death) refers to an inescapable, pre-ordained end, aligning with pre-Islamic concepts of fate (manāyā) as a cosmological force.
"لعمرك!" is a common pre-Islamic oath formula, swearing by the life of the addressed person, which functions as a sacred entity in the oath.
This verse expresses the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of inescapable fate or destiny, where an ultimate end (like death or capture) is unavoidable, even if temporarily delayed.
This verse contains the oath formula 'لعمرك' (by your life), which is a common pre-Islamic oath anchored to a sacred entity (life itself).
The term 'الدهر' (Time/Fate) is invoked here as a cosmological force that apportions events, reflecting a key pre-Islamic belief about fate.
The term 'نصب' (nuṣub) explicitly refers to a standing stone or idol, a common feature of pre-Islamic Arabian sanctuaries and ritual spaces.
The term 'أيسار' refers to individuals who performed divination by casting lots, thus representing a specific sacral role in pre-Islamic Arabian society.
This verse explicitly mentions 'fulfilling a vow' (توفي... نذرا), which refers to the pre-Islamic practice of making and fulfilling votive offerings or promises to deities or sacred entities.

The verse explicitly mentions 'رفعوا المنيح' (they raised the manīḥ), referring to the pre-Islamic practice of casting lots with arrows (maysir) to determine fortune or division of goods, which falls under divination.
The term 'الدهر' (dahr) is used, referring to time or fate as a significant, often impersonal, cosmological force in pre-Islamic belief.
The term 'الدهر' (dahr) is personified as an agent that affects human life, aligning with its role as a cosmological force in pre-Islamic thought.
This verse describes swearing an oath at a 'naṣb,' a pre-Islamic standing stone or altar, which served as a sacred entity for such ritualistic oaths.
This verse describes a woman's wailing (النحيب) over a slain hero, which is an explicit reference to pre-Islamic mourning rites and lamentation practices.
The verb "قدرن" (they were decreed/destined) implies a pre-ordained fate for the journeys, aligning with pre-Islamic cosmological concepts of destiny.
The verse explicitly mentions 'الأزلام' (divinatory arrows), a well-known pre-Islamic practice for casting lots and seeking guidance.
This verse explicitly mentions 'al-anṣāb' (sacred standing stones) and the ritual act of 'shedding blood' (sacrifices) between them, indicating pre-Islamic cultic practice.
The verse compares the assembly to a 'ḥaram', which was a sacred, inviolable precinct in pre-Islamic Arabia, indicating a reference to sacred geography and ritual space.
